lens.name: Educational psychology
lens.discipline: learning science and the social psychology of educational sorting
questions: How might the proposal affect learning, cognitive load, motivation, self-regulation, academic self-concept, identity, and aspiration?
Do selection, ranking, labeling, or reference-group mechanisms create psychological benefits or costs for selected and non-selected students?
Which claimed mechanisms are robust, context-dependent, or contested?
limitations: Big-fish-little-pond effects are robust at group level but do not determine any individual student's outcome.
Labeling and teacher-expectancy effects are contested in magnitude.
Stereotype-threat effects are contested, affected by publication bias, and must not be generalized beyond the tested population and domain.
Growth-mindset intervention effects are small and heterogeneous; the lens must not treat them as a universal remedy.
Psychological effects must not be used to override legal, fiscal, equity, or democratic value judgments.

evidence_notes.claim: Equally able students tend to develop lower academic self-concept in higher-achieving selective environments, a robust cross-national reference-group effect with implications for aspiration.
evidence_notes.source: Marsh and subsequent cross-national replications, including PISA samples

evidence_notes.claim: Teacher-expectancy and labeling effects exist but are contested in magnitude; average effects are smaller than early Pygmalion claims and may be larger for stigmatized or low-SES students.
evidence_notes.source: Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) and later replication literature

evidence_notes.claim: Stereotype-threat effects in performance settings are generally smaller and less robust than early literature suggested and show evidence of publication bias.
evidence_notes.source: Flore and Wicherts (2015); Shewach, Sackett and Quigley (2019)

evidence_notes.claim: Structures emphasizing ranking and selection can promote performance orientation and fear of failure, while mastery orientation is associated with persistence; developmental timing matters.
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness support more self-determined motivation; controlling environments can undermine it.
Average effects of growth-mindset interventions on academic achievement are small and heterogeneous, so they should not be proposed as a structural substitute.
evidence_notes.source: Sisk et al. (2018) meta-analysis and subsequent debate
